audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 18, first-pass draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for military organization, 'worth ten thousand' leader valuation, David's public protection order, twenty-thousand deaths, forest/terrain casualties, Absalom's entrapment, refusal of unlawful reward, Joab's execution and subordinates, dishonored burial, monument tradition, Cushite messenger identity, evasive reporting, and David's grief.

verses.restored: David mustered people with him and placed over them commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds.

David sent people: one third by hand of Joab, one third by hand of Abishai son of Zeruiah, brother of Joab, and one third by hand of Ittai the Gittite. King said to people, Going out, I will also go out with you.

People said, You shall not go out, because if we flee they will not set heart on us; if half of us die they will not set heart on us. Now you are like ten thousand of us. It is better now that you be for us from city to rescue/support.
King said to them, What is good in your eyes I will do. King stood at side of gate, and all people went out by hundreds and thousands.
King commanded Joab, Abishai, and Ittai, saying, Gently for my sake with young man, with Absalom. All people heard when king commanded all commanders concerning Absalom.
People went out to field against Israel, and battle was in forest of Ephraim.
People of Israel were struck there before servants of David. Slaughter there was great on that day: twenty thousand.
Battle spread there over face of all land, and forest devoured more among people than sword devoured on that day.

Absalom encountered servants of David. Absalom rode upon mule. Mule came under tangled branches of great tree, and his head was caught in tree. He was left between heavens and earth, while mule under him passed on.
A man saw and told Joab, saying, Look, I saw Absalom hanging in tree.
Joab said to man who told him, Look, you saw—why did you not strike him there to ground? I would have given you ten silver pieces and a belt.

Man said to Joab, Even if I weighed upon my palms one thousand silver pieces, I would not stretch out my hand against son of king. In our ears king commanded you, Abishai, and Ittai, saying, Guard for me young man Absalom.
Or I would have acted falsely against his life/my life, and no matter is hidden from king; you yourself would have stood apart against me.
Joab said, I will not wait thus before you. He took three staffs/darts in his hand and thrust them into heart/body of Absalom while he was still alive in heart of tree.
Ten young men, bearers of Joab's armor, surrounded, struck Absalom, and put him to death.
Joab blew shofar, and people returned from pursuing Israel, because Joab restrained people.
They took Absalom, threw him into a great pit in forest, and raised over him a very great heap of stones. All Israel fled, each to his tent.

Absalom in his lifetime had taken and raised for himself a pillar in valley of king, because he said, I have no son to cause my name to be remembered. He called pillar by his name, and it is called Monument of Absalom until this day.
Ahimaaz son of Zadok said, Please let me run and announce to king that YHWH judged/rescued him from hand of his enemies.
Joab said to him, You are not a man of news this day. You may announce another day, but this day you shall not announce, because son of king is dead.
Joab said to the Cushite, Go, tell king what you saw. Cushite bowed to Joab and ran.
Ahimaaz son of Zadok again said to Joab, Whatever may be, please let me also run after Cushite. Joab said, Why should you run, my son, when there is no rewarding news for you?
Whatever may be, let me run. He said, Run. Ahimaaz ran by way of plain and passed Cushite.
David sat between two gates. Watchman went to roof of gate by wall, lifted his eyes, saw, and look, a man running alone.
Watchman called and told king. King said, If alone, news is in his mouth. He came nearer and nearer.
Watchman saw another man running. Watchman called to gatekeeper and said, Look, a man running alone. King said, This one also brings news.
Watchman said, I see running of first like running of Ahimaaz son of Zadok. King said, He is a good man and comes with good news.
Ahimaaz called and said to king, Peace. He bowed to king with face to ground and said, Blessed be YHWH your Divine, who delivered men who lifted their hand against my lord king.
King said, Is there peace for young man Absalom? Ahimaaz said, I saw a great tumult when Joab sent servant of king and your servant, but I do not know what.
King said, Turn aside and stand here. He turned aside and stood.
Look, Cushite came. Cushite said, Let my lord king receive news, because YHWH judged/rescued you today from hand of all who rose against you.
King said to Cushite, Is there peace for young man Absalom? Cushite said, May enemies of my lord king and all who rise against you for evil be like young man.
King trembled, went up to upper room over gate, and wept. Thus he said as he went: My son Absalom, my son, my son Absalom. Who would give that I had died in your place, Absalom, my son, my son.

translation_notes: The claim that David is 'worth ten thousand' values the ruler over thousands of ordinary lives. Strategic importance does not establish greater human worth.
David's protection order is public and clear. Joab's killing of Absalom directly violates it.

The twenty thousand dead are participants in a civil war, not an anonymous obstacle to David's restoration. 'The forest devoured' may include terrain, pits, exposure, and chaotic flight rather than supernatural vegetation.
The Hebrew says Absalom's head was caught; it does not explicitly say his famous hair caused the entrapment. Moralizing vanity through hair is unwarranted.
The unnamed soldier refuses Joab's reward because he heard the king's order and anticipates Joab would abandon him. Refusal of an unlawful command is morally significant.
Joab wounds Absalom while suspended and ten armor-bearers complete the killing. This is execution of a trapped person, not fair combat.
Absalom's burial in a pit and stone heap degrades the defeated body. His monument and child tradition contain textual tension.
The Cushite messenger's ethnicity must not become a racial stereotype or servant caricature. He bears the truth Ahimaaz avoids.
David's lament is authentic parental grief, but wishing to die in Absalom's place is not a suicide model or proof he should have ignored the lives of his troops.
safety_note: Do not glorify civil-war casualties, rank a leader as more human than thousands, blame Absalom's hair, or praise Joab's insubordination. David's death-wish is grief language, not instruction. Center the refused unlawful order, the trapped young man, the messengers, the dead across both armies, and a father's devastating loss.
